g.text: Process Synchronization

g.data-narration: Process synchronization manages shared resource access in multi-process environments, preventing race conditions that cause data corruption and system failures in concurrent programs.
g.text: 1. What is Process Synchronization?
Process synchronization manages access to shared resources in multi-process environments,
preventing race conditions that cause data corruption and unpredictable behavior in concurrent programs.
Essential for maintaining data consistency and correct program execution.

g.data-narration: The critical section problem requires solutions satisfying three essential requirements for correct concurrent execution: mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting in operating systems.
g.text: 2. Critical Section Problem
Critical section is code accessing shared variables that must not be accessed concurrently.
Three requirements must be satisfied for proper synchronization:
• Mutual Exclusion • Progress • Bounded Waiting

g.data-narration: Hardware atomic instructions like test-and-set, compare-and-swap, and swap provide indivisible operations for implementing efficient synchronization primitives in modern processors.
g.text: 3. Hardware Solutions
Modern processors provide atomic instructions executing as single uninterruptible units.
• Test-and-Set: Atomically tests and modifies a memory location
• Compare-and-Swap: Updates value only if it matches expected value

g.data-narration: Peterson's algorithm is a software solution for two processes using shared flags and turn variable to achieve mutual exclusion without special hardware support.
g.text: 4. Software Solutions
Peterson's Algorithm: Software-based solution for two processes using shared variables.
Uses two flags and a turn variable for coordination and mutual exclusion.
Limitation: Only works for two processes, assumes atomic memory accesses.

g.data-narration: Synchronization primitives include mutexes for mutual exclusion, semaphores for counting access, and monitors for high-level synchronization with automatic mutual exclusion.
g.text: 5. Synchronization Primitives
• Mutex: Provides mutual exclusion, allowing only one thread to acquire lock
• Semaphore: Integer with wait() and signal(); allows multiple concurrent accesses
• Monitor: High-level construct with automatic mutual exclusion

g.data-narration: Key exam points: remember the three critical section requirements, differentiate hardware from software solutions, and understand mutex versus semaphore differences in operating systems.
g.text: 6. Exam Tips
Remember: Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting | Differentiate hardware vs software
Know mutex vs semaphore difference | Classic problems frequently asked in exams
